title: The Modern AI Stack: Demystifying the Architecture of Artificial Intelligence
thesis: Artificial intelligence is not a single monolithic product but a modular stack of distinct engineering layers; understanding the layers helps builders and observers place tools, companies, and research advances in context.
primary_reader: A technically-curious reader who encounters AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude Desktop, or open-source models and wants a map of how the pieces fit together.
intended_outcome: Readers should be able to place a tool or research announcement into the correct layer of the AI stack and understand why much of the industry's engineering focus has shifted toward orchestration and tool protocols.
artifact_fit: An article fits because the value is a durable conceptual model and shared vocabulary, not a one-off product announcement or code snippet.
smallest_viable_version: The nine-layer model plus the mapping table, with each layer summarized in one paragraph and tied to a public example.
scope_risks: The article could become an exhaustive product catalog or a stale benchmark comparison. It should stay at the conceptual layer, label the model as the author's proposed taxonomy, and let the mapping table carry the examples.
alternatives_considered: A single diagram or a glossary; the layered narrative was chosen because it gives readers a decision framework, not just definitions.
claims: Modern AI systems can be usefully understood as a modular stack rather than a single monolithic model.
Base-model scaling remains important, but much of the visible product engineering and investment has moved up the stack toward orchestration and tool protocols.
Retrieval-augmented generation is a common pattern for grounding models in proprietary or real-time data.
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is emerging as a common adapter for agent-tool integration.
End-user applications such as Claude Desktop bundle multiple lower layers into a single GUI.

sources.title: Nous Research Hermes Agent on GitHub

sanitized_summary:
A conceptual tour of the modern AI stack organized into nine layers, from compute
hardware and base models through alignment, inference, retrieval, orchestration,
tool protocols, guardrails, and end-user applications. The article uses public
products and protocols as anchor examples and argues that much of the industry's
engineering focus has shifted up the stack toward agentic orchestration and tooling.
